preamble: The shift from the lateral [l] to a retroflex stop (like [ɖ] or [ʈ]) is a form of fortition and assimilation. It typically occurs when a lateral is influenced by an adjacent rhotic or through the simplification of liquid-dental clusters in languages with retroflex phonology.

examples.note: Tamil/Malayalam contrast the retroflex lateral ḷ (ள) with dental l (ல). In some Tamil dialects and positions, the retroflex lateral ḷ merges with or approximates [ɖ]. Distinct Dravidian feature not found in IE.
Retroflex assimilation: the sequence of /r/ and /l/ merges into a single retroflex sound.
Historical reduction of liquid-stop clusters into a single retroflex stop.
